title: Renașterea libertății: Istoria Societății Mont Pèlerin. Interviu cu Stefan Kolev
summary: An interview with economist Stefan Kolev about the history of the Mont Pèlerin Society (MPS), tracing its origins from the 1938 Walter Lippmann Colloquium through its 1947 founding, and examining how liberal intellectuals sought to reinvent liberalism after its perceived failures in the 1930s. The article explores internal ideological tensions between neoliberals and paleo-liberals, the society's shift toward American-style economics under Friedman's generation, and whether a similar intellectual revival could happen today.
talking_points: The Mont Pèlerin Society and the Walter Lippmann Colloquium were critical responses to liberalism's perceived failure in the 1930s, gathering roughly 50% of all self-declared liberal intellectuals of that era

The society experienced a fundamental shift from broad ordoliberal thinking integrating social, political, and cultural dimensions to a narrower American-style economic liberalism under Friedman and Stigler
MPS's greatest success was having a ready-made policy program when Keynesianism failed in the 1970s, enabling direct influence on Reagan and Thatcher
A revival of MPS-style energy would require both acknowledgment of recent failures and a new positive liberal program that is radical in substance but empathetic in language
author: Redacția
